1.synthesis:
Covenant renewal begins with Moses cutting two new stone tablets — a significant difference from the first set, which God himself had cut (Exod 31:18). Matthew Henry notes: "Before, God himself provided the tables, and wrote on them; now, Moses must hew out the tables, and God would only write upon them. Thus, in the first writing of the law upon the heart of man in innocence, God did all; in the second writing after apostasy, man is concerned in it." The renewed covenant cost human labor on its human side; the divine side still supplied the writing.

Moses ascended alone — "No one shall come up with you, and let no one be seen throughout all the mountain" (Exod 34:3). Even the flocks must not graze near the mountain. The solitude of the second ascent marked its character: this was a covenant restoration requiring the mediator alone, without the distraction or contamination of the assembled people who had so recently failed.

At the summit, God descended in the cloud, stood with Moses, and proclaimed his name. The proclamation of Exodus 34:6-7 is perhaps the most important theological statement in the entire OT: "The Lord, the Lord, a God merciful and gracious, slow to anger, and abounding in steadfast love and faithfulness, keeping steadfast love for thousands, forgiving iniquity and transgression and sin, but who will by no means clear the guilty, visiting the iniquity of the fathers on the children and the children's children, to the third and the fourth generation." This is the divine self-definition that the rest of Scripture will quote, echo, and develop more than any other single passage — the DNA of biblical theology of God.

Moses's response is immediate prostration and renewed intercession: "If now I have found favor in your sight, O Lord, please let the Lord go in the midst of us, for it is a stiff-necked people, and pardon our iniquity and our sin, and take us for your inheritance" (Exod 34:9). Moses prays the people's stubbornness not as a reason for God to keep distance but as a reason to come near: the stiff-necked people most need a God who will not leave them to themselves.
1.historical_context: The divine name-proclamation of vv 6-7 becomes the most-cited passage in the OT: it is directly quoted or closely echoed in Num 14:18, Neh 9:17, Ps 86:15, Ps 103:8, Ps 145:8, Joel 2:13, Jonah 4:2, and Nah 1:3, among others. It functions as Israel's canonical definition of who God is — the baseline theological formula that every subsequent description of divine character presupposes. The pairing of "merciful and gracious" with "who will by no means clear the guilty" holds together what cheaper theologies separate: God's compassion never excuses sin, and his judgment never operates without mercy.
1.literary_structure: The passage moves from preparation (Moses cuts the tablets, vv 1-4) through divine descent and name-proclamation (vv 5-7) to human response (Moses prostrates and intercedes, vv 8-9). The name-proclamation is the theological center, framed by the two acts of descent (God, v 5) and prostration (Moses, v 8). The intercession of v 9 brings the passage to rest on the same note as Moses's earlier prayers: covenant, forgiveness, inheritance.
1.theological_application: The name-proclamation of vv 6-7 has been called "the most important text in the OT" — a summary of biblical theology in two verses. It holds together what systematic theologies sometimes separate: the attributes of mercy and justice, compassion and holiness. God is "slow to anger" — not slow to see, not indifferent — but his anger does not fire quickly; his compassion is "abounding" while his holiness is absolute. Matthew Henry comments: "What an excellent mixture is here of justice and mercy!" This characterization of God grounds every act of divine forgiveness in the OT and ultimately finds its resolution at the cross, where God is both "just and the justifier of the one who has faith in Jesus" (Rom 3:26).
1.christology: Paul's reading of this passage in 2 Corinthians 3:7-18 — comparing the glory of the old covenant (fading) with the greater glory of the new — shows that the divine self-proclamation of Exodus 34 is ultimately about Christ, in whom all of God's "steadfast love and faithfulness" is fully manifest (John 1:14). The "steadfast love" (ḥesed) and "faithfulness" (ʾemet) of v 6 are the exact words John uses of the Word incarnate: "grace and truth." In Christ the divine name proclaimed on Sinai is embodied and completed: the one who "by no means clears the guilty" is the one who also "forgives iniquity, transgression, and sin" — both attributes satisfied in the one cross, where justice and mercy kissed (Ps 85:10).

10.synthesis:
God responds to Moses's intercession by ratifying a new covenant: "Behold, I am making a covenant. Before all your people I will do marvels, such as have not been done in all the earth or in any nation" (Exod 34:10). The covenant stipulations that follow (vv 11-27) repeat and intensify the core prohibitions of Israel's earlier covenant obligations, with special emphasis on the danger of Canaanite religious influence. The sequence covers prohibition against covenants with the land's inhabitants (vv 12-13), destruction of their altars and sacred poles (v 13), prohibition of other gods (vv 14-16), no cast idols (v 17), and then the great calendar of festivals: Unleavened Bread, Firstfruits, Sabbath, Weeks, and the three pilgrimage feasts (vv 18-24).

Keil and Delitzsch observe that the covenant renewed here is not substantively different from the original Sinai covenant — the same God, the same people, the same stipulations — but it is a restoration after breach rather than an inaugural agreement. The covenant texts of vv 11-26 echo the Book of the Covenant (Exod 20-23) in compressed form, functioning as a "covenant summary" rather than a new legislation. Gill notes that the focus on separation from Canaanite religion (vv 12-16) was particularly urgent in light of what had just happened with the golden calf — Israel had already demonstrated the danger of adopting foreign religious forms.

Moses was on the mountain forty days and forty nights, "neither eating bread nor drinking water" (Exod 34:28), writing the covenant words on the tablets. The forty-day fast parallels the first Sinai experience (Exod 24:18) and anticipates Elijah's forty-day journey to the same mountain (1 Kgs 19:8) and Jesus's forty-day fast in the wilderness (Matt 4:2). The covenant renewal required a full repetition of the original encounter's cost — forty days, absolute fast — indicating that the restoration was not cheap but required complete recommitment from the human mediator.
10.historical_context: The covenant text of vv 11-26 is sometimes called the "Ritual Decalogue" in critical scholarship because it emphasizes cultic regulations. It is better understood as a selective summary of covenant obligations particularly relevant to the golden calf crisis: Israel had just shown that they could not resist the pull of borrowed religious forms, so the renewed covenant emphasizes precisely the prohibition of alien worship. The three-times-annual male appearance before God (v 23) — at Unleavened Bread, Weeks, and Booths — is a practical assertion of Israel's covenant identity as a nation gathered around the God who brought them out of Egypt, not around the agricultural deities of Canaan.
10.literary_structure: The covenant renewal (vv 10-28) moves from God's declaration (v 10), through the covenant stipulations (vv 11-26), to Moses's obedient response — writing on the tablets (vv 27-28). The stipulations emphasize separation from Canaanite religion (vv 11-17), then the calendar of covenant feasts (vv 18-26). The structure mirrors the original Decalogue and Book of the Covenant in miniature: prohibitions first, then the positive schedule of covenant observance. The forty-day conclusion (v 28) closes the Sinai encounter symmetrically with its opening.
10.theological_application: The renewed covenant after Israel's catastrophic failure is a declaration that God is the God of restoration. The first tablets shattered at the mountain's base were not the end of the story; God does not abandon covenant when his people break it. His initiative — "I am making a covenant" (v 10) — drives the renewal, not Israel's remorse or improvement. This is the pattern of all scriptural restoration: "If we are faithless, he remains faithful — for he cannot deny himself" (2 Tim 2:13). The covenant's focus on separation from the surrounding culture's religion speaks to the perennial danger that Gill identifies: Israel's greatest threat was not military conquest by Canaan but spiritual absorption into Canaanite religious life — the slow loss of distinctiveness through accommodation.
10.christology: The "marvels" God promises to do in the renewed covenant (v 10) are ultimately fulfilled in Christ: "the works that I do in my Father's name bear witness about me" (John 10:25). The forty-day fast that enables Moses to receive the renewed covenant points to Jesus's forty-day fast in the wilderness — the second Moses who fasted not to receive the old covenant written on stone but to prepare for his own ministry as the mediator of the new. As Moses returned from Sinai with the written covenant in stone, Jesus returned from the wilderness "in the power of the Spirit" (Luke 4:14), equipped to write the new covenant on human hearts. The prohibition of all rival gods and sacred poles in the renewed covenant anticipated the total displacement of every competing loyalty by the claim of Christ: "No one can serve two masters" (Matt 6:24).

29.synthesis:
When Moses descended from Sinai carrying the two tablets of the renewed covenant, his face was shining — radiant with a light he himself did not perceive: "Moses did not know that the skin of his face shone because he had been talking with God" (Exod 34:29). Aaron and the people of Israel were afraid to come near him. Moses called them back and spoke to them; then he put a veil over his face (Exod 34:33). Thereafter, whenever Moses went in before the Lord to speak with him, he removed the veil; when he came out and spoke to Israel, he put it back on — until the next encounter took it off again.

Keil and Delitzsch track the Hebrew verb carefully: the word used for Moses's face shining (qāran) derives from the root meaning "to send forth rays," as horns project from a surface. It is this verb that produced Jerome's Vulgate translation cornuta ("horned"), which in turn produced Michelangelo's famous horned Moses. But the context is unambiguous: the radiance was light, not horns — the physical afterglow of extended divine encounter. Adam Clarke notes that the Hebrews themselves consistently understood it as luminous glory: "the brightness was such that it dazzled the eyes and terrified the people."

Paul's interpretation in 2 Corinthians 3:7-18 is the definitive Christian reading: the glory on Moses's face was real but fading, and the veil was placed over it not to hide the brilliance but "so that the Israelites might not gaze at the outcome of what was being brought to an end." The veil was a mercy — preventing Israel from seeing the glory diminish — and a symbol of the old covenant's inherent impermanence. By contrast, "we all, with unveiled face, beholding the glory of the Lord, are being transformed into the same image from one degree of glory to another" (2 Cor 3:18). The veil has been removed in Christ.
29.historical_context:
The shining of Moses's face is unique in the OT narrative — no other human is described as physically luminous from divine encounter, though the burning bush, the pillar of cloud and fire, and the glory at Sinai all manifest divine light. The phenomenon connects to the "glory cloud" (šekhīnāh) that filled the tabernacle in Exodus 40 — the same divine radiance that transformed Moses's face. Adam Clarke records that the root qāran is associated with projecting light in Job 16:15 and Habakkuk 3:4, confirming the luminous rather than literal-horn reading.

The veil Moses wore when speaking to the people was removed for divine encounter and replaced for human encounter — a behavioral pattern that inverted ordinary intuition (one might expect to cover one's face before the holy and display it before the people). But Moses was a mirror, not a lamp: the shine was not his own, and it faded. The veil regulated public exposure of what belonged to God.
29.literary_structure: The passage (vv 29-35) closes both chapter 34 and the golden calf narrative arc of chs 32-34. The arc that began with Israel's shame (ch 32) ends with Moses's glory. The veil practice (vv 33-35) is structured as a regular rhythm: in before God (veil off) → out to people (veil on) → back in (veil off again) — a repeated cycle of alternating revealed/hidden glory that Paul uses as the structural basis for his 2 Corinthians 3 argument.
29.theological_application:
The shining face of Moses establishes the principle that extended time in God's presence changes the person in visible ways. Moses did not know his face was shining — which means the effect was not self-generated. Those who spend time with the living God are changed in ways they themselves may not perceive; it is others who see the difference. Paul's application is universal: "we all, with unveiled face, beholding the glory of the Lord, are being transformed into the same image" (2 Cor 3:18). What happened once to Moses at Sinai is now the ongoing experience of every believer who turns to the Lord: transformation "from one degree of glory to another."

The fading of Moses's glory — the veil worn partly to conceal the diminishing light — speaks to the old covenant's transience. It was genuinely glorious, but its glory was temporary. The new covenant brings a permanent, increasing glory: "if there was glory in the ministry of condemnation, the ministry of righteousness must far exceed it in glory" (2 Cor 3:9).
29.christology:
Paul reads the shining face of Moses as a direct anticipation of the gospel's ministry. The light that transformed Moses's face was the same divine glory that shines "in the face of Jesus Christ" (2 Cor 4:6). At the transfiguration, Jesus's face "shone like the sun" (Matt 17:2) — not with borrowed, fading light but with the full, permanent radiance of divine sonship. Moses himself appeared at the transfiguration, a final OT witness to the one whose glory fulfills and surpasses what Moses had briefly reflected on his own face.

The removal of the veil "in Christ" (2 Cor 3:14) is the hermeneutical key Paul draws from this passage: the veil that hid the fading glory of the old covenant is removed when one turns to Christ, the one in whom the glory is unfading. The church reads the OT with unveiled face — seeing in Moses's shining face not the summit of revelation but its anticipation, pointing forward to the face of the One before whom Moses himself had trembled.
